topics.name: Future Tenses (will, going to, Present Continuous)
Used to / Would / Be used to

Reported Speech: Your friend said: 'I am going to the cinema tonight.' Tell me what your friend said using reported speech.
Tell me about advice someone gave you recently. Use reported speech.
Your mother said: 'I can't find my phone.' Report this to me.
John said: 'I will call you tomorrow.' Report it in past tense.
Your boss told you: 'You must finish the report today.' What did your boss say?
A stranger asked: 'Where is the train station?' Report that question in reported speech.
Your friend asked: 'Do you want to come with me?' Tell me what they asked.
Think of a recent conversation. Tell me two things the other person said, using reported speech.
Your doctor said: 'You should drink more water.' Report it.
Your teacher told the class: 'The test is next Monday.' Report that.
Your sibling said: 'I don't like this movie.' Report it.
Someone once told you something that changed your mind. Tell me what they said using reported speech.
A waiter said: 'The kitchen is closing in ten minutes.' Report it to your friend.
Report this question: 'What time does the meeting start?'
Your grandmother said: 'Life was simpler when I was young.' Report this to someone.
Past Simple vs Past Continuous: Tell me about a time something unexpected happened while you were doing something else.
What were you doing at 8 p.m. last Saturday? Did anything happen?
Describe a day last week. What were you doing when a friend called or messaged you?
Tell me about an accident or near-miss you witnessed or experienced.
What were you wearing yesterday? What did you do during the day?
Describe a time you met someone interesting. What were they doing when you first saw them?
Tell me what happened the last time you were cooking and got interrupted.
Describe a moment from your last holiday. What were you doing and what happened next?
Tell me about your morning today. What were you doing when you realised the time?
Describe a memorable childhood moment using both past simple and past continuous.
Tell me about a phone call that interrupted you while you were doing something important.
Describe the weather yesterday. What did you do during the day?
Tell me about the last storm or heavy rain you experienced. What were you doing?
Describe a moment when you were deep in thought and someone startled you.
Tell me about a time you arrived somewhere and something unusual was happening.
Present Perfect vs Past Simple: Tell me three things you have done this week and one thing you did last weekend.
Have you ever travelled to another country? If yes, when did you go and what did you do?
What is the most interesting book you have ever read? When did you read it?
Tell me about a skill you have learned recently. How did you start?
Have you eaten any new food this month? What did it taste like?
What is something you have always wanted to do but haven't done yet?
Tell me about a film you have seen more than once. When did you first watch it?
Have you met anyone famous? Describe that experience in detail.
What have you done today? What did you do yesterday?
Describe a place you have visited many times and one you visited only once.
Tell me about the best concert or event you have ever attended.
Have you ever lost something important? What happened?
What is a challenging thing you have done recently?
Tell me about a friendship that has lasted a long time. When did it start?
Have you ever moved to a new city? When and why?
Conditionals (0, 1st, 2nd, 3rd, Mixed): What will you do if it rains tomorrow?
If you could live in any country, which one would you choose and why?
What would you have done differently if you had known about an important event in your life?
If you win the lottery next week, how will you spend the money?
What do you do if you can't sleep at night?
If you were the leader of your country for one day, what would you change?
Imagine you missed your flight yesterday. What would you have done?
If someone offers you a job abroad, will you accept it?
What would your life be like now if you had chosen a different career?
If you could have dinner with any historical figure, who would it be and what would you ask?
If water boils at 100°C, what happens to ice at 0°C? Explain using zero conditional.
If you were invisible for a day, what would you do?
If you hadn't learned English, how would your life be different today?
What will you do if the weather is good this weekend?
If you could change one decision from your past, what would it be and why?
Passive Voice: Describe how a traditional dish from your country is made, using the passive voice.
Tell me about a famous building in your city. When was it built and by whom?
Explain how a book you love was written — who wrote it and when was it published?
Describe a process you know well (making coffee, sending a letter) using the passive voice.
Tell me about a gift you were given recently. Who was it given by?
Describe something that was stolen or lost. What happened?
Tell me about a festival that is celebrated in your country.
How are children taught to read in your country?
Describe a product that is made in your region.
Tell me about a film that was directed by someone you admire.
Describe how paper is made, step by step.
Tell me about a song you love — when was it released and who was it written by?
Describe a sport: how is it played? What are the rules?
Tell me about an invention that changed the world. When was it invented?
Describe how bread is made in a traditional bakery.
Modal Verbs (must, should, could, might): What should a person do to stay healthy? Give me at least three pieces of advice.
What must you do every day at work or school?
You could travel anywhere tomorrow. Where might you go and why?
What should students do to learn English faster?
Tell me about something you could do when you were a child but can't do now.
What must people do to take care of the environment?
What might happen in your country in the next ten years?
What should I do if I lose my passport while travelling?
Give three rules visitors to your home must follow.
What could you do to improve your English this month?
You might be offered a job in another country. What would you consider?
What advice should parents give their teenagers?
What must a good leader do? What shouldn't they do?
What could go wrong on a long journey? How should you prepare?
What should people do when they feel stressed?
Future Tenses (will, going to, Present C: What are you doing this weekend? Tell me about your plans.
What do you think will happen in technology in the next twenty years?
What are you going to do after this conversation?
Describe your plans for your next holiday.
What will you do when you finish your studies or retire from work?
Tell me about a goal you are going to achieve this year.
What are you meeting people for this week?
Predict three things that will happen in your country next year.
What are you going to eat for dinner tonight, and why?
When will you next see your family? What are you going to do together?
What are you planning to buy next month?
Imagine a normal day one year from now. What will it look like?
What are you doing tomorrow morning?
How will artificial intelligence change your job in the future?
What are you going to do to improve your English in the next three months?
Articles (a / an / the / zero): Describe your favourite room in your home. Use articles carefully.
Tell me about a book and a film you enjoyed recently.
Describe a typical day in your life from morning to evening.
Tell me about the most beautiful place you have visited.
Describe a person in your family — what do they do, where do they live?
Tell me about a restaurant you like. What do they serve?
Describe an animal you find interesting.
Tell me about a sport you play or watch.
Describe the weather in your country across the four seasons.
Tell me about a hobby that makes you happy.
Describe the street where you live.
Tell me about a teacher who had an impact on you.
Describe an object on your desk right now.
Tell me about the first time you saw the sea.
Describe a tool or piece of technology you use every day.
Prepositions of Time and Place: Where do you usually eat breakfast, and at what time?
Describe the location of your home — what is near it?
Tell me what you do on weekends, in the morning, at night.
Where do you go on holiday, and when do you usually travel?
Describe your workplace or classroom — where are things located?
Tell me about the city you live in — what is it between, next to, or near?
When is your birthday, and what do you do on that day?
Where did you go last weekend and at what time?
Describe a photo on your wall — who is in it and where?
Tell me about a journey you took. Where did you go, and when?
Describe your morning routine — at what time do you do each thing?
Where do you usually study or work? What is around you?
Describe a park or garden near your home.
When do you feel most energetic during the day?
Tell me about a shop you visit often. Where is it?
Comparatives and Superlatives: Compare your hometown with another city you know well.
What is the best film you have ever seen, and why is it better than others?
Compare two members of your family — who is taller, more patient, etc.?
Tell me about the most difficult thing you have ever done.
Compare summer and winter in your country.
Which is more useful for learning English: books or videos? Why?
Tell me about the most delicious meal you have ever eaten.
Compare your life now with your life five years ago.
What is the biggest challenge facing young people today?
Compare two jobs you could do — which one is harder, more interesting?
Compare reading a book and watching a film.
What is the most surprising place you have visited?
Compare two restaurants you know well.
Who is the kindest person you know, and why?
Compare life in a big city and life in a small town.
Relative Clauses (who, which, that): Describe a person who has influenced you. Use 'who' or 'that'.
Tell me about a gift which you really loved.
Describe the place that makes you happiest.
Tell me about a book whose ending surprised you.
Describe a friend who always makes you laugh.
Tell me about a film which changed the way you think.
Describe a teacher who inspired you.
Tell me about something that you own which is very important to you.
Describe a city that you would like to visit.
Tell me about a moment in your life which you will never forget.
Describe a meal which is typical of your country.
Tell me about a song that always cheers you up.
Describe a person whose work you admire.
Tell me about an object that has sentimental value.
Describe a day that was perfect from start to finish.
Gerunds and Infinitives: What do you enjoy doing in your free time? What do you want to do next year?
Tell me something you love doing and something you hate doing.
What have you decided to do about your English studies?
Tell me about a habit you need to stop and one you want to start.
What do you find difficult to understand? What do you avoid doing?
Tell me about someone who taught you to do something important.
What are you planning to do this weekend? What do you feel like doing right now?
Tell me about a skill you keep trying to improve.
What do you remember doing as a child that you miss?
What do you hope to achieve by learning English?
Tell me about something you would like to try.
What do you enjoy about your current routine?
What have you promised yourself to do this year?
Describe something worth doing in your city.
Tell me about a goal that requires working hard every day.
Phrasal Verbs: Tell me about the last time you ran into an old friend.
What time do you usually get up, and what do you do to wake up properly?
Describe a problem you recently worked out.
Tell me about a plan that fell through.
How do you get on with your neighbours or colleagues?
Tell me about something you gave up — a habit or activity.
What do you usually get up to on Sunday afternoons?
Describe the last time you dressed up for a special occasion.
Tell me about a time you had to put up with something difficult.
What time did you turn in last night, and when did you wake up?
Tell me about a relationship you broke off or that ended.
Describe something you have taken up recently as a new interest.
Tell me about a word or phrase you recently looked up.
Describe a situation where you had to calm down.
Tell me about plans you had to call off last minute.
Question Tags: You're enjoying our conversation, aren't you? Continue — tell me why.
Ask me three questions using question tags.
Make a statement about the weather today and add a question tag.
Comment on something about yourself and check it with a question tag.
Tell me what you think about English, using question tags to confirm.
Describe your last meal and ask me about mine using question tags.
Make statements about your home city and add question tags.
You haven't been to Japan, have you? Answer and continue.
Tell me about your morning and use two question tags.
Check with me what you know about me — use question tags.
You can speak more than one language, can't you? Tell me about it.
You don't like waking up early, do you? Explain.
Make three statements about this year and confirm each with a tag.
Describe your family using question tags to check with me.
You're going to continue learning English, aren't you? Why?
Used to / Would / Be used to: Tell me three things you used to do as a child that you don't do now.
What did you use to eat as a child that you don't eat anymore?
Describe how life used to be before mobile phones.
Tell me about something you are used to doing every morning.
What would you do every summer when you were young?
Tell me about a habit you had to get used to at school or work.
Describe how your hometown used to look ten years ago.
What sports did you use to play? Do you still play them?
Tell me about something you aren't used to yet.
Describe how people used to travel a hundred years ago.
What music did you use to listen to that you don't listen to now?
Tell me about a place you used to visit often.
What have you recently got used to?
Describe family traditions you used to follow as a child.
Tell me a story your grandparents used to tell you.
General Conversation: Tell me about yourself — who you are, what you do, and what you enjoy.
What does a typical weekend look like for you?
Describe the place where you live.
Tell me about a recent happy memory.
What are your goals for this year?
Describe your best friend and why you get along.
What is your favourite way to relax after a busy day?
Tell me about a skill or hobby you'd like to learn.
What is something that made you smile this week?
Tell me about a place you want to visit and why.
Describe your favourite season of the year.
What is something you do to stay motivated?
Tell me about a small thing that brings you joy.
Describe a challenge you overcame recently.
Job Interview Practice: Tell me about yourself and your professional background.
Why are you interested in this position?
What are your greatest strengths, and how do they help you at work?
Describe a weakness you are actively working to improve.
Tell me about a challenging project you completed successfully.
Where do you see yourself in five years?
Describe a time you had a conflict at work and how you resolved it.
Why are you leaving your current job?
How do you handle pressure and tight deadlines?
Do you have any questions for us? Ask three.
Describe a time you led a team or took initiative.
What motivates you in your career?
Tell me about a time you failed and what you learned.
How do you prioritise competing tasks?
Why should we hire you instead of other candidates?
Travel and Daily Situations: You have just arrived at the airport. Ask for directions to your hotel.
Order a meal at a restaurant — starter, main, and drink.
You lost your luggage. Explain the problem to the airline staff.
Book a taxi to the city centre for 7 p.m. tomorrow.
Ask a stranger for help finding the nearest pharmacy.
Check into a hotel and ask about Wi-Fi, breakfast, and check-out time.
You want to return something you bought in a shop. Explain why.
Buy a train ticket — specify destination, time, and type of ticket.
Describe a problem with your hotel room and ask for a solution.
Make a reservation at a restaurant for four people on Saturday night.
You missed your bus stop — ask the driver what to do.
Ask a local about the best places to visit in their city.
Report a lost phone at a police station.
Rent a car — specify how long, what type, and insurance.
Ask for help using a vending machine or ticket kiosk.
Opinions and Debate: Should children have smartphones? Give your opinion with reasons.
Is social media a good thing for society? Explain your view.
Do you think working from home is better than working in an office?
Should junk food be taxed more heavily? Why or why not?
Is it important to learn more than one language? Justify your opinion.
Should university education be free for everyone? Debate both sides.
Are electric cars really better for the environment? Share your view.
Do you think money can buy happiness? Explain.
Should people be required to vote in elections?
Is it better to have a small close group of friends or many casual friends?
Do you think space exploration is worth the cost? Why?
Should animals be kept in zoos? Give your reasoning.
Is technology making us less social? Defend your position.
Do you think traditional books will disappear?
Should companies have a four-day work week?
Storytelling and Memories: Tell me the story of your most memorable birthday.
Describe a time you were really scared. What happened?
Tell me about the funniest thing that ever happened to you.
Describe your first day at a new school or job.
Tell me about a journey that didn't go as planned.
Describe a time a small decision changed your life.
Tell me about meeting someone who became very important to you.
Describe a tradition from your childhood that you miss.
Tell me about a time you helped someone in need.
Describe your happiest memory in detail.
Tell me about a lesson you learned the hard way.
Describe a time you felt truly proud of yourself.
Tell me about an adventure you had as a child.
Describe your favourite place from your childhood.
Tell me about a time you did something brave.

Reported Speech: Change time and place words: 'today' → 'that day', 'here' → 'there', 'now' → 'then'.
Use 'told + someone' for statements and 'asked' for questions. Drop question word order in reported questions.
Past Simple vs Past Continuous: Use past continuous for the longer background action and past simple for the shorter interrupting action.
'While' and 'as' usually go with past continuous; 'when' often introduces past simple.
For completed actions in the past, stick with past simple. Don't overuse '-ing' forms.
Present Perfect vs Past Simple: Use present perfect for life experiences with no specific time ('I have visited Rome').
Use past simple when you mention a finished time ('I visited Rome last year').
Signal words: ever, never, yet, already, just → present perfect; yesterday, ago, in 2010 → past simple.
Conditionals (0, 1st, 2nd, 3rd, Mixed): 1st conditional = real future (If it rains, I will stay). 2nd = imaginary present (If I had money, I would travel).
3rd conditional = imagined past (If I had studied, I would have passed).
Avoid mixing 'will' and 'would' inside the same clause — keep one tense pattern.
Passive Voice: Form: subject + be (correct tense) + past participle (+ by + agent if needed).
Use passive when the doer is unknown or unimportant, or when you want to emphasise the action.
Match the tense of 'be' to the time of the action: 'is made', 'was built', 'has been written'.
Modal Verbs (must, should, could, might): After modals, use the base verb — no 'to' and no '-s': 'She should go', not 'She should to go'.
For past: 'should have done', 'could have gone', 'might have missed it'.
Future Tenses (will, going to, Present C: Use 'going to' for plans decided before now; 'will' for on-the-spot decisions or predictions.
Use present continuous for fixed arrangements with a time ('I'm meeting Sara at 6').
Avoid 'will' for planned events you already organised.
Articles (a / an / the / zero): Use 'a/an' when you mention something for the first time, 'the' when the listener knows which one.
No article with most proper nouns, meals, languages, and general plural/uncountable nouns.
Use 'the' with unique things (the sun), superlatives (the best), and most rivers/oceans.
Prepositions of Time and Place: 'At' for points (at 7 p.m., at the door), 'on' for surfaces/days (on Monday), 'in' for enclosed spaces and long periods (in 2026).
Memorise set phrases: 'at night', 'on the weekend' (US) / 'at the weekend' (UK), 'in the morning'.
Comparatives and Superlatives: Short adjectives add '-er/-est' (tall → taller → the tallest).
Long adjectives take 'more/most' (interesting → more interesting → the most interesting).
Irregular: good → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst; far → further → the furthest.
Relative Clauses (who, which, that): 'Who' for people, 'which' for things, 'that' works for both in defining clauses.
In non-defining clauses (extra information) use commas and avoid 'that': 'My brother, who lives in Paris, ...'.
Don't double the subject: 'The man who he called' → 'The man who called'.
Gerunds and Infinitives: After prepositions always use the gerund ('interested in learning', not 'to learn').
Some verbs take gerunds (enjoy, avoid, finish); others take infinitives (want, decide, hope).
A few verbs change meaning with each form: 'stop smoking' ≠ 'stop to smoke'.
Phrasal Verbs: Phrasal verbs are idiomatic — learn them in context, not in lists.
Some are separable ('turn it off'), others inseparable ('run into her'). Pronouns usually go in the middle of separable ones.
Try to replace formal verbs with phrasal verbs to sound natural: 'continue' → 'carry on', 'reject' → 'turn down'.
Question Tags: Positive statement → negative tag; negative statement → positive tag.
Match the auxiliary: 'She can swim, can't she?' — with no auxiliary, use 'do/does/did': 'You like it, don't you?'.
Falling intonation = checking known info; rising intonation = real question.
Used to / Would / Be used to: 'Used to + base verb' = past habit or state that is no longer true.
'Be/get used to + -ing' = being accustomed to something (present/any tense).
'Would + base verb' describes repeated past actions, NOT past states.
General Conversation: Link your ideas with connectors like 'because', 'so', 'however', and 'for example'.
Use a mix of tenses to sound natural: talk about your past, present routine, and future plans.
Don't rush. Short clear sentences are better than long broken ones.
Job Interview Practice: Structure answers with the STAR method: Situation, Task, Action, Result.
Quantify achievements where you can ('increased sales by 20%').
Avoid filler words. Pause briefly instead of saying 'um'.
Travel and Daily Situations: Use polite forms: 'Could I...', 'Would you mind...', 'I'd like...'.
Learn set phrases for each situation (checking in, ordering, asking directions).
Confirm important information by repeating it back: 'So that's a double room for two nights, correct?'.
Opinions and Debate: Start with a clear position: 'In my opinion...', 'I strongly believe...'.
Give reasons and examples: 'for instance', 'because', 'this shows that'.
Acknowledge the opposite view before defending yours: 'while some argue..., I think...'.
Storytelling and Memories: Set the scene first (when, where, who) before jumping into events.
Mix past simple (main events) with past continuous (background) for natural storytelling.

defaultTips: Practice speaking aloud every day, even if only for five minutes.
Record yourself and listen back to notice mistakes you don't catch while speaking.
Build vocabulary around topics you actually want to talk about.
